steps.title: Spot the comma splice
steps.content:
The sentence contains two independent clauses separated only by a comma:

Clause 1: “This conceptualization of harmonic opposites did not originate with Collier in 1985”
Clause 2: “the idea was first articulated in Levy's book”

A comma alone cannot join two independent clauses—this is a comma splice error.

steps.title: Apply the semicolon rule
steps.content:
Two independent clauses need a semicolon or period between them — a comma alone is a comma splice. Insert each choice and read the full sentence:
  • A — "Collier, though": "…did not originate with Collier, though in 1985, the idea was first articulated…" — comma before "though" leaves two independent clauses joined only by a comma = comma splice. ✗
  • B — "Collier, though;": "…did not originate with Collier, though; in 1985, the idea was first articulated…" — the semicolon after "though" separates the two clauses correctly. "Though" here works as a parenthetical concession (like "admittedly"), modifying clause 1. The semicolon then properly joins the two independent clauses. ✓
  • C — "Collier, though,": "…did not originate with Collier, though, in 1985, the idea was first articulated…" — two commas around "though" treat it as a parenthetical, but the two independent clauses are still joined only by commas = comma splice. ✗
  • D — "Collier; though,": "…did not originate with Collier; though, in 1985, the idea was first articulated…" — the semicolon is in the wrong place (mid-subject of clause 1), splitting the sentence before "though" makes "though, in 1985" an awkward fragment opener. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
